title: Cat Pupils Different Sizes? Causes and Emergency Steps for Anisocoria
meta_desc: If your cat has pupils of different sizes, it could be a medical emergency. Learn about anisocoria and why you should see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Anisocoria, or unequal pupil size, is a high-priority veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention. It can indicate serious conditions such as head trauma, glaucoma, or neurological disorders that may lead to permanent blindness.

What is anisocoria in cats and what does it look like?

Anisocoria is a condition where a cat's pupils are noticeably different in size. One pupil may be dilated (wide), while the other is constricted (small). This imbalance suggests that something is interfering with the nerves or muscles that control the iris, or there is an issue within the eye itself. It is a clinical sign of an underlying problem rather than a specific disease.

Is unequal pupil size in my cat a medical emergency?

  • Yes, unequal pupil size is considered a high-priority emergency that requires immediate contact with your veterinarian or an emergency animal hospital.
  • The urgency is high because this symptom can be caused by severe issues such as head trauma, glaucoma, internal eye inflammation (uveitis), or neurological disorders.
  • If left untreated, the underlying causes can lead to permanent blindness or indicate life-threatening conditions.

How can taking a photo of my cat’s eyes help with veterinary triage?

  • If your cat is stable enough to sit for a moment, capture a clear, high-resolution photo of their eyes in a well-lit room.
  • A photo provides an invaluable baseline for the triage nurse or doctor to see exactly how the eyes looked at the onset.
  • The image helps the veterinary team determine if the condition is changing, worsening, or responding to light by the time you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Anisocoria in cats can be caused by a variety of conditions including dysautonomia, systemic hypertension, intraocular hemorrhage, neoplasia (pituitary masses, paranasal masses, meningiomas, lymphosarcoma), granulomatous meningoencephalomyelitis, trauma, and orbital cellulitis. In cases of suspected dysautonomia, clinical confirmation may be aided by contrast radiography of the esophagus and reduced lacrimal secretion. Pilocarpine (0.1%) applied to the cornea causes profound miosis within 10-15 minutes due to denervation hypersensitivity. Dilute (0.5%) phenylephrine can reverse ptosis and a protruding third eyelid.

Chapter: Neurology, Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: Does anisocoria mean my cat is in pain?
faqs.answer: While the pupil size change itself might not be painful, many of the underlying causes, such as corneal ulcers or glaucoma, are very painful for cats and require urgent care.

faqs.question: Can my cat recover from different sized pupils?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends entirely on the underlying cause. If the issue is caught early and treated effectively, many cats can recover or manage the condition.

faqs.question: Are there other symptoms I should look for?
faqs.answer: Yes, watch for eye redness, cloudiness, squinting, discharge, or changes in your cat's balance and behavior, as these are critical details for your vet.
